findings.message: bulk_create_items commits inside the for-loop, so the batch is not atomic: if any iteration fails (constraint violation, connection drop, etc.), earlier items remain committed while later ones never persist. This violates the all-or-nothing invariant callers expect from a 'bulk' write and leaves the store in an inconsistent partial state that cannot be rolled back.
findings.suggestion: Add all items first, then commit once outside the loop (e.g. `for name in names: session.add(Item(name=name))` then a single `session.commit()` / `session.flush()` after the loop), or wrap the whole batch in `with session.begin():` so a single transaction covers the entire insert and a failure rolls back every row.

findings.message: Committing per row inside a loop also amplifies write amplification (one transaction + fsync per item) and, combined with `session.refresh(item)`, issues an extra round-trip per row — turning what should be a single bulk insert into N transactions. Beyond the atomicity bug, this is a correctness-adjacent concern because any retry/idempotency strategy the caller layers on top will see partial state.
findings.suggestion: Use a single transaction for the whole batch; if generated PKs/defaults are needed for the return value, rely on `session.flush()` (still in-transaction) rather than committing each row.
